metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC #1's proving command to use two separate imports: `from rebar.llm.plan_review import registry as r` and `import json` on separate lines (or use a semicolon), so the command does not raise ImportError.
evidence: "from rebar.llm.plan_review import registry as r, json"
Standard library `json` must be imported as `import json` (top-level), not as a member of `rebar.llm.plan_review`; the comma-form only works when `json` is an attribute of the package.
findings.finding: AC #1's proving command will raise ImportError before it can assert anything: `from rebar.llm.plan_review import registry as r, json` attempts to import the standard-library `json` module from the `rebar.llm.plan_review` package namespace, which does not export it — this is not a valid multi-name import from a package.
scenarios: An agent runs the proving command verbatim; it fails with ImportError on the import line, making the criterion appear unverifiable even when the registration is correct.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the first line of the python -c block with:
`from rebar.llm.plan_review import registry as r; import json`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC2's proof command) that explicitly verifies `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` passes and names what threshold-recalibration logic it covers.
evidence: Scope files list includes `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py`
AC2 proof: `pytest tests/unit/test_decomp_shape_criterion.py -q` exits 0 — references only test_decomp_shape_criterion.py
No other AC references test_threshold_recalibration.py
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` is listed in the Scope/Files section as a named deliverable but no AC verifies its content, behavior, or that it passes — the only test-proof command in AC2 runs `pytest tests/unit/test_decomp_shape_criterion.py -q`, leaving this file as uncovered work.
scenarios: Implementer authors test_threshold_recalibration.py but it is never run in CI verification, silently breaks, or is omitted entirely — no AC catches either outcome.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2's proof to `pytest tests/unit/test_decomp_shape_criterion.py tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py -q` and add one sentence describing what recalibration logic the latter file tests (e.g., that adding decomp-shape does not shift existing criterion thresholds unexpectedly).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the multi-bin information-loss gap for `decomp-shape`: either (a) specify that the rubric must be written to work from roster-name evidence alone and document the reduced-recall trade-off for multi-bin decompositions, OR (b) route `decomp-shape` as a single whole-decomposition call (receiving all children at once) separate from the per-child G3/G4 fan-out, accepting the new routing path as the mechanistic cost of a whole-partition-axis criterion.
evidence: pass1_container signature: `children: list[dict]` (current bin only), `sibling_roster: str` (name+id only, passes.py:407: `## Complete sibling roster (for absence cross-check)\n{sibling_roster}`)
pack_container_bins (sizing.py:48-76) splits large decompositions across multiple bins, each receiving a subset of children whole
Layer-cake smell requires seeing ALL children's partition axes; consumed-artifact smell requires both producer+consumer children's full text — neither is guaranteed in a multi-bin scenario
Plan asserts 'the complete sibling roster' covers this, but the roster is only title+id (pass1.py:154: `roster = '\n'.join(f'- {c.get(ticket_id)}: {c.get(title, '')}' for c in ctx.children)`)
G3/G4 are inherently per-child (coverage/consistency evaluated child-by-child), so the bin-pack limitation is benign for them — decomp-shape is architecturally different
findings.finding: The `decomp-shape` criterion is a WHOLE-decomposition partition-axis audit, but the existing container seam evaluates criteria per-BIN — each `pass1_container` call receives only its packed children as full text; other siblings appear in the roster as name+id strings only. For layer-cake detection (requires seeing ALL children's partition axes) and consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge detection (requires seeing both producer and consumer children's full text), multi-bin fan-outs silently reduce the evidence available to the model. The plan describes the seam as providing 'parent + whole children + the complete sibling roster' but in reality the other-bin children are only available as thin names — a false-negative risk the plan does not acknowledge or mitigate.
findings.location: What §Mechanism seam; pass1.py:166 (pack_container_bins); passes.py:357-455 (pass1_container); plan phrase 'hands the finder parent + whole children + the complete sibling roster'
scenarios: A 5-child epic with 4 large children gets bin-packed into 4 single-child bins; each call sees only 1 child + 4 roster name entries. The layer-cake split (DB/service/frontend/test/infra) is undetectable from one child + 4 names.
Producer child A (outputs a schema file) and consumer child B (imports that schema) land in different bins; the consumed-artifact smell cannot be reliably detected from name-only roster entries.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile R3's E2 gate elision with the parent epic's rule: either (a) update the parent epic to formally accept hand-authored sanity fixtures as a valid substitute for E2 on advisory-only criteria (requires parent re-review), or (b) invoke the INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE — document E2's cost-rejection as the formal failing result and close R3 deferred, shipping it only after a re-run or explicit parent-level exception is recorded.
evidence: Parent epic 6982 integration record comment: 'R3=3a27 (decomposition-shape, advisory-only, gated E2+E6)'
Parent epic 6982 AC: 'no detection-bearing recommendation implemented without its eval gate'
Parent epic 6982 Context: 'INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE: ... every dependent R-story closes deferred with the results recorded — ambiguity never rounds up to a pass'
R3 Scope OUT: 'the E2/E3 mutant corpus (permanently rejected on cost); sanity uses hand-authored fixtures'
R3 does not invoke the INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE — it ships the criterion rather than closing/deferring
findings.finding: R3 ships a detection-bearing criterion without its parent-designated E2 eval gate, neither passing E2 nor following the parent's INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE (which requires closing/deferring with the failing result recorded, not shipping sans gate with redefined fixtures).
findings.location: Scope section ('OUT: ... the E2/E3 mutant corpus (permanently rejected on cost)') vs. parent epic 6982 AC ('no detection-bearing recommendation implemented without its eval gate') and integration record ('R3=3a27 ... gated E2+E6')
scenarios: An implementer executes R3 as written and ships decomp-shape without E2 validation; the parent's gate rule is violated and R3's effectiveness is unvalidated against the seeded-defect benchmark E2 was designed to provide.

A reviewer auditing epic 6982's AC compliance finds R3 landed without its designated eval gate and without a recorded INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE deferral — the AC 'no detection-bearing recommendation implemented without its eval gate' is violated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief 'E2 gate disposition' subsection to R3's Scope that explicitly invokes the INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE: record E2 (d125) as 'not runnable on cost grounds' as the formal failing result, note that R3 therefore ships under the INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE's deferral path but with advisory-only posture as the safe fallback, and update the parent epic's AC comment to acknowledge this disposition. This keeps the parent's protocol intact while documenting the exception transparently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `sibling_roster` is assembled in the eval_solver container-arm (e.g., derived from the fixture's `children` list titles, an explicit fixture field, or empty-string for sanity fixtures) and confirm this matches the production cross-check behavior.
evidence: Plan: 'This story extends that arm to run a container criterion via pass1_container when the fixture carries a children: list — a small, contained enhancement'
passes.py:364: `sibling_roster: str` is a required keyword-only parameter of pass1_container
eval_solver._run_criterion_case currently uses only `case.get('input')` — no sibling_roster assembly logic exists
AC #3: 'the eval_solver container-arm (a fake runner threads through pass1_container)' — depends on this working correctly
ABSENCE: the plan does not state the fixture schema for sibling_roster (empty string? derived from the children list? a separate fixture field?)
findings.finding: The eval_solver container-arm extension is described as 'a small, contained enhancement' that threads `pass1_container` when a fixture carries a `children:` list, but `pass1_container` requires a `sibling_roster` string parameter (passes.py:364) that in the live path is built from the ticket graph. The plan does not specify how `sibling_roster` is populated from a YAML fixture in the eval path — leaving an unverified capability assertion about the ease of the extension, which the bounded sanity harness (AC #3) directly depends on.
findings.location: What section — 'Bounded sanity harness'; AC #3; eval_solver.py:105 extension description
scenarios: The eval_solver extension is implemented with `sibling_roster=''` (or omitted), causing pass1_container to behave differently from the live path (the sibling cross-check that prevents false absence findings is skipped), so sanity fixture results are not representative of production behavior

The YAML fixture format for the sanity eval (`plan-review-decomp-shape.eval.yaml`) does not include a sibling_roster field and the extension silently passes an empty roster, causing false positives on the 'must-not-fire' fixtures
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `sibling_roster` field to the eval fixture schema (or specify it is auto-derived as the joined child titles/ids from the `children` list) and document this in the plan's fixture-shape description and AC #3's proof statement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the container-eval fixture schema in the plan: specify the YAML field names for the parent plan text, children list (including per-child required fields: `ticket_id`, `title`, `description`), and how the eval_solver constructs `sibling_roster` from the fixture's child list for the `pass1_container` call.
evidence: Current eval_solver.py:105-109: raises ValueError for any `cid in CONTAINER_CRITERIA` with message 'needs a ticket graph / session log'. The plan says to extend this arm when 'the fixture carries a children: list' — but the exact field name and child object schema are unspecified.

passes.pass1_container signature (passes.py:357-365): requires `parent_plan: str`, `children: list[dict]`, `criteria: list[dict]`, `sibling_roster: str`. The mapping from fixture fields to these parameters is not specified.

The asserted-capability analogue fixture (plan-review-asserted-capability.eval.yaml) uses `input:` for the inline plan text — but for container criteria, `input:` would be the parent and `children:` would need a new field.
AC3 specifies 'hand-authored fixtures — one known layer-cake container plan + one known consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge plan' but gives no schema for how children are represented in the YAML.
findings.finding: The eval_solver container-arm extension is described ('extends that arm to run a container criterion via pass1_container when the fixture carries a children: list') but the fixture schema for container eval cases is never specified: what fields carry the parent plan text (`input:` vs `plan:`?), what fields carry children (a `children:` list of what shape?), how the sibling roster is constructed from fixture data, and how the attribution output (`_container_child`) is validated. The AC2 unit test spec ('a fake runner threads through pass1_container') requires this schema to be unambiguous, and the sanity fixture (AC3) at `plan-review-decomp-shape.eval.yaml` must conform to it — but without the schema, the fixture shape is implementation-defined.
findings.location: What section — 'Bounded sanity harness'; AC2 — 'the eval_solver container-arm (a fake runner threads through pass1_container)'; Scope/Files — `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_solver.py`
scenarios: Implementer uses `parent:` for the parent plan and `children:` for child list; unit test uses `input:` and `kids:`; the sanity fixture uses a third schema — three divergent shapes that all 'thread through pass1_container' by accident of test setup.

The sibling_roster is constructed as a newline-joined list of child titles by the solver — but the rubric relies on ticket_ids in the roster for cross-check. Without specifying this, the container eval fires false-positives because the roster is malformed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fixture schema note: container eval cases use `parent: <str>` for the plan text and `children: [{ticket_id, title, description}, ...]` for the child list; the solver constructs `sibling_roster` as `'\n'.join(f'- {c["ticket_id"]}: {c["title"]}' for c in children)` (matching the production pass1.py:154 pattern).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the closed vocabulary (`{yes|no|insufficient}` or `{yes|no}`) for each of `has_container_decomposition` and `decomposition_shape_sound` in the rubric body (`plan_review_decomp_shape.md`), matching the `asserted-capability`/`removal-rationale` precedent, and include `insufficient` semantics (e.g., 'insufficient when the child set is ambiguously structured but not clearly layered').
evidence: Plan: 'has_container_decomposition (GATE — parent has >=2 children forming a decomposition; no -> not-applicable -> PASS) and decomposition_shape_sound (no when a layer-cake partition OR a consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge is present — the finding)' — no vocabulary enumeration.
asserted-capability rubric body (plan_review_asserted_capability.md:52-58): explicitly states `{yes|no|insufficient}` for each sub-answer key.
removal-rationale rubric body (plan_review_removal_rationale.md:40-47): explicitly states `{yes|no|insufficient}` for each sub-answer.
`_criterion_block` at passes.py:243-247 renders `c.get('checklist')` keys+checks into the prompt — so the vocabulary MUST appear in the rubric's checklist spec to reach the model.
The rubric file `plan_review_decomp_shape.md` is listed in Scope/Files but its body content is never described in the plan.
findings.finding: The sub-answer ENUM VOCABULARY for the two criterion-local checklist sub-answers (`has_container_decomposition`, `decomposition_shape_sound`) is never defined. Analogous criteria (`asserted-capability`: `{yes|no|insufficient}`, `removal-rationale`: `{yes|no|insufficient}`) specify their closed three-value vocab in the rubric body, and `_criterion_block` (passes.py:243-247) renders that checklist into the prompt so the model knows which literals are valid. The plan says `no` gates/fires but does not specify whether the vocabulary is two-value (`yes|no`) or three-value (`yes|no|insufficient`), nor what `insufficient` means for each sub-answer — the rubric file content is entirely unspecified.
findings.location: What section — 'Two criterion-local checklist sub-answers'; Scope/Files — `reviewers/plan_review_decomp_shape.md`
scenarios: The implementing author writes a two-value (yes/no) vocabulary; the model occasionally produces `insufficient` (matching the T8/asserted-capability precedent) and the gate coerces it to a fail-safe default that either always passes or always fires — systematic miscalibration not caught until the sanity run.

The checklist entry in criteria_routing.json lacks a `check` description (since the plan doesn't specify it), so _criterion_block renders an empty/generic check bullet and the model has no basis for distinguishing the gate state.
findings.suggested_fix: In the rubric file body, add a CHECKLIST SUB-ANSWERS section like: `has_container_decomposition {yes|no} — GATE: does the parent have >=2 named children forming a decomposition? no → not-applicable → PASS. decomposition_shape_sound {yes|no|insufficient} — only when gated in: is the decomposition free of layer-cake partitioning AND consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge? no → finding; insufficient when the child descriptions are too sparse to determine the partition axis.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the rubric body (`plan_review_decomp_shape.md`) the operational detection rules for both smells: for layer-cake, define what partition axis makes a split 'by layer' (e.g., ALL children address only one tier with no user-observable outcome deliverable alone) vs. a valid vertical slice; for consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge, define what constitutes 'naming/consuming an artifact' (file path, symbol, schema, event type) and what ordering-edge evidence is sufficient to suppress the finding.
evidence: Plan Why section defines smells in motivation prose only; the rubric file body is listed in Files but its decision rules are not specified anywhere in the plan.

plan-review-finder.eval.yaml:209: 'layers/files is the SHAPE OF A GOOD vertical slice, not a decomposition trigger' — showing the FP boundary exists and is known but not incorporated into the rubric spec.

`_criterion_block` at passes.py:243-247 renders `c.get('scenario', '')` from the rubric into the prompt instructions — the scenario IS the decision rule the model reads. If the plan does not specify the scenario body, the implementer must invent the decision rules.
G4's rubric (plan_review_G4.md) explicitly names all 7 interaction modes in the scenario body — the precedent for co-locating decision rules with the schema.
findings.finding: The detection decision rules for the two decomposition smells are defined only in the plan's Why section (prose motivation), never co-located with the rubric schema the LLM will actually read. Specifically: (1) 'layer-cake split' is defined as 'children partitioned by ARCHITECTURAL LAYER' but the rubric body is unspecified — the model has no operational definition of when a partition is by 'layer' vs. a legitimate vertical slice. The existing G5 eval corpus (plan-review-finder.eval.yaml:209) explicitly notes 'layers/files is the SHAPE OF A GOOD vertical slice, not a decomposition trigger' — the FP boundary is plan-known but not rubric-specified. (2) 'consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge' requires detecting when child B 'names/consumes an artifact a sibling produces' — 'names' and 'consumes' are undefined operationally (file path reference? symbol import? schema field reference?).
findings.location: Why section — 'Two recurring decomposition-SHAPE smells'; Scope/Files — `reviewers/plan_review_decomp_shape.md` (body unspecified)
scenarios: Implementer writes a layer-cake detection rule that fires on any child touching a 'service' or 'API' layer regardless of whether it also delivers user-visible behavior — high FP rate on real vertical-slice decompositions that happen to touch multiple layers.

The consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge rule fires whenever any child mentions a file that appears in another child's description, regardless of whether there is a real artifact dependency — FP on co-located code paths.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `'decomp-shape': (0.95, 'advisory')` to `EXPECTED_ROUTING` in `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` (the full-table regression pin at line 136 will break if omitted when `decomp-shape` is added to `criteria_routing.json`).
evidence: Plan Scope/Files: 'tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py' listed as an in-scope file
test_threshold_recalibration.py:136: `assert actual == EXPECTED_ROUTING` — a full-table exact-equality pin over all CANONICAL_LLM criteria
EXPECTED_ROUTING (lines 33-72) does not include 'decomp-shape'; adding decomp-shape to criteria_routing.json without updating EXPECTED_ROUTING breaks this assertion
Plan nowhere mentions updating EXPECTED_ROUTING or the pinned table
findings.finding: The plan names `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` in its Scope/Files list but does not acknowledge that `test_full_routing_table_matches_pinned_expectation()` (line 136) asserts `actual == EXPECTED_ROUTING` — a pinned complete routing table that does NOT include `decomp-shape`. Adding `decomp-shape` to `criteria_routing.json` (as required by every AC) will break this existing test; the plan contains no instruction to add `"decomp-shape": (0.95, "advisory")` to `EXPECTED_ROUTING`.
scenarios: Developer lands the decomp-shape criterion per the plan's ACs; pytest test_threshold_recalibration.py immediately fails with an extra-key mismatch on 'decomp-shape', breaking CI.
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py`, add `"decomp-shape": (0.95, "advisory")` to the `EXPECTED_ROUTING` dict. Also verify whether `test_only_the_eleven_approved_criteria_are_blocking` (line 139) needs updating — it checks the blocking set is exactly `LOWERED | PROMOTED | {"T4"}`; since decomp-shape is advisory it will not appear there, so that assertion should still pass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan body what entry must be added to `EXPECTED_ROUTING` in `test_threshold_recalibration.py` (i.e., `'decomp-shape': (0.95, 'advisory')`) so the full-table regression pin stays passing after the `criteria_routing.json` addition.
evidence: tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py:133-136: `def test_full_routing_table_matches_pinned_expectation(): ... assert actual == EXPECTED_ROUTING`
EXPECTED_ROUTING dict (lines 33-72) lists every current criterion by id; 'decomp-shape' is absent

Plan 'Files' list includes `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` but plan body says only 'deterministic (no-LLM) unit tests' in test_decomp_shape_criterion.py — the needed change to test_threshold_recalibration.py is never specified
findings.finding: test_threshold_recalibration.py contains a full-table regression pin (test_full_routing_table_matches_pinned_expectation) that asserts the COMPLETE routing dict equals a hardcoded EXPECTED_ROUTING map. Adding 'decomp-shape' to criteria_routing.json will add a new key absent from EXPECTED_ROUTING, breaking this pinned test. The plan lists the file in 'Files' but the plan body never describes what change is required.
scenarios: Developer runs `pytest tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` after adding the decomp-shape routing entry; test_full_routing_table_matches_pinned_expectation fails because 'decomp-shape' is a new key not in EXPECTED_ROUTING
CI fails the unit test suite even though the plan's AC2 proof command only names test_decomp_shape_criterion.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add `'decomp-shape': (0.95, 'advisory')` to the `EXPECTED_ROUTING` dict in `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` and note this in the plan's Files/edit list.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean, improve, sufficient
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete jq assertion to AC #3's Proof, e.g. `jq -e '[.cases[] | select(.fixture_type=="positive") | .fired] | all' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/decomp_shape_sanity.json` (exits 0 only when every positive fired), or equivalent, so the recall==1.0 target is machine-checkable.
evidence: "records recall over the positives (target 1.0)"
No jq or grep command is given to assert that the committed decomp_shape_sanity.json actually encodes recall==1.0; a reviewer must manually inspect the file.
findings.finding: AC #3 states a recall target of 1.0 over the positive fixtures but provides no concrete assertion command to verify that target from the committed JSON — 'preserves the raw per-case output and records recall over the positives (target 1.0)' is descriptive, not executable.
scenarios: A committed JSON that records recall=0.5 (one missed positive) satisfies the AC as written because no command checks the value; the gate silently fails.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to AC #5, e.g. `grep -E 'decomp-shape' docs/plan-review-gate.md && grep -iE 'fleiss_kappa|order.stability|E6' docs/plan-review-gate.md` (both lines must exit 0) or `rebar explain decomp-shape` plus a grep on the gate doc.
evidence: "Proof: the section names `decomp-shape` and the E6 order-stability prerequisite."
No grep, rebar explain, or structured assertion is given to confirm the section exists with the required content.
findings.finding: AC #5 describes what the documentation section must CONTAIN but provides no concrete proving command to verify it in-session — 'Proof: the section names `decomp-shape` and the E6 order-stability prerequisite' is a prose description, not an executable check.
scenarios: An agent marks the criterion done after adding any text to plan-review-gate.md; no automated check catches a section that names only `decomp-shape` but omits the E6 prerequisite note, or vice-versa.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC #5's Proof line: `grep -q 'decomp-shape' docs/plan-review-gate.md && grep -qiE 'fleiss_kappa|order.stability' docs/plan-review-gate.md && echo OK`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either remove `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` from the Files list if it requires no changes for this story, or add an AC (with proving command) describing what threshold-recalibration logic is touched and what the tests must assert.
evidence: "tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py" appears in the Files list
No AC references this file; AC #2's pytest command covers only `test_decomp_shape_criterion.py`; no 'What' section describes changes to threshold recalibration logic.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` appears in the Files scope list but has no acceptance criterion, no description of what changes are required, and no proving command — an executor has no actionable guidance on what to write or test there.
scenarios: An agent skips the file (no AC maps to it) or writes arbitrary tests; either outcome satisfies all ACs, leaving the intent unverified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either enumerate `fidelity_spot_eval.py` in the plan's Files list and note it intentionally excludes decomp-shape from the fidelity spot-eval (with rationale), or update `fidelity_spot_eval.py:216` to derive the container list from `CONTAINER_CRITERIA` so decomp-shape is automatically included.
evidence: fidelity_spot_eval.py:216: `container = [registry.by_id()['G3'], registry.by_id()['G4']]` — hardcoded, not derived from CONTAINER_CRITERIA
plan Files list does not mention fidelity_spot_eval.py
pass1.py:373-374 partitions using CONTAINER_CRITERIA (correct seam), but fidelity_spot_eval.py bypasses this
findings.finding: fidelity_spot_eval.py:216 hardcodes `container = [registry.by_id()['G3'], registry.by_id()['G4']]` — it does NOT derive the container criteria list from CONTAINER_CRITERIA. Adding decomp-shape to CONTAINER_CRITERIA will not auto-include it in the fidelity spot-eval path. This caller is outside the plan's named file set and is an unenumerated consumer of the container-criteria set.
scenarios: A developer running the fidelity spot-eval after adding decomp-shape expects it to also bench the new criterion's packing fidelity, but it silently runs only G3/G4
If a future story tries to validate decomp-shape packing fidelity, the spot-eval would need a separate update

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py` to the plan's Files list (or note explicitly that its `pass1_container` docstring referencing `G3+G4` must be updated to reference the full `CONTAINER_CRITERIA` set).
evidence: passes.py:374-376: docstring says 'Run ALL container criteria (G3+G4)' and 'out-of-set tags DROPPED, a finding mapping to no in-set criterion dropped (mirrors pass1_chunk; never fabricate an attribution). The container prompt describes both audits over the shared (parent, children, roster) context; presenting both rubrics'
passes.py is absent from the plan's Files list
findings.finding: passes.py docstring at line 375 hardcodes the container criterion set as '{G3,G4}' in two places. After adding decomp-shape to CONTAINER_CRITERIA and the rubric, this docstring will be stale. The file passes.py is not listed in the plan's Files section.
scenarios: Post-merge, the docstring describes only G3+G4 while the function now runs G3+G4+decomp-shape, misleading future developers
findings.suggested_fix: Update the `pass1_container` docstring from 'Run ALL container criteria (G3+G4)' to 'Run ALL container criteria (currently G3, G4, decomp-shape)' and reference CONTAINER_CRITERIA rather than hardcoding ids.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly acknowledge whether existing tests asserting `eval_solver` raises on `CONTAINER_CRITERIA` must be updated/retired, and add a rollback note (e.g., 'revert eval_solver.py:105 branch; re-raise unconditionally') in case the container-arm extension regresses existing eval workflows.
evidence: 'eval_solver._run_criterion_case raises for cid in CONTAINER_CRITERIA, eval_solver.py:105. This story extends that arm to run a container criterion via pass1_container when the fixture carries a children: list'
Files list includes test_decomp_shape_criterion.py for the NEW arm, but no mention of updating/retiring existing tests that guard the raise behavior
No rollback or back-out plan stated for the eval_solver behavioral change
findings.finding: The plan modifies eval_solver.py:105 to change existing behavior (currently raises for CONTAINER_CRITERIA) to a new branch (run via pass1_container when fixture carries children:), but does not acknowledge whether existing tests that assert the raise behavior exist and would break, and provides no rollback/back-out plan if the eval_solver extension introduces regressions in existing eval workflows.
findings.location: ## What — 'Bounded sanity harness' paragraph; eval_solver.py:105; AC item 3
scenarios: An existing test suite asserts eval_solver raises for CONTAINER_CRITERIA; the extension silently breaks those tests without the plan covering the update
If the pass1_container integration inside eval_solver misbehaves, there is no documented rollback path to restore the raise behavior
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the 'Bounded sanity harness' paragraph: 'Any existing test asserting the raise-on-CONTAINER_CRITERIA behavior will be updated to assert the new branch path instead. Rollback: revert the eval_solver branch to unconditional raise, removing the children: dispatch, with no impact on production plan-review paths.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Quantify the promotion thresholds in `docs/plan-review-gate.md`: specify numeric floors for `detection_proxy`, `blocking_fp_proxy`, and minimum sample size (e.g., matching whatever d8a5's protocol specifies, or explicitly setting them here) so the advisory→blocking gate is objectively measurable.
evidence: Plan text: 'must show it earns its blocks (high detection_proxy, low blocking_fp_proxy, sufficient sample)'
No numeric floors for detection_proxy, blocking_fp_proxy, or sample size are given anywhere in the plan
Contrast: the E6 floor IS quantified (fleiss_kappa=0.55 < 0.6 floor, raw=0.7 < 0.8)
findings.finding: The advisory→blocking promotion gate names qualitative conditions ('high detection_proxy, low blocking_fp_proxy, sufficient sample') that are never quantified. Without numeric floors, the gate documented in docs/plan-review-gate.md is unmeasurable — a future operator cannot objectively decide when promotion is warranted.
findings.location: docs/plan-review-gate.md §'Standing per-criterion effectiveness recorder + advisory->blocking promotion gate'; also AC line 5 ('citing d8a5's protocol')
scenarios: Operator reviews effectiveness data after dogfood accumulation and cannot determine whether the criterion qualifies for blocking promotion
Two reviewers disagree on what 'high' and 'sufficient' mean, causing gate to be ignored or arbitrarily triggered
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table to the gate section: e.g., detection_proxy ≥ 0.80, blocking_fp_proxy ≤ 0.10, n ≥ 30 firings — numbers calibrated to match d8a5's protocol or chosen explicitly here and cross-referenced.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for the eval_solver container-arm extension: define whether a failed live call aborts the whole sanity run (fail-fast, no partial JSON committed) or retries with backoff, and ensure the committed decomp_shape_sanity.json is either complete or absent — never partial.
evidence: Plan text: 'This story extends that arm to run a container criterion via pass1_container when the fixture carries a children: list'
No mention of retry, timeout, or partial-result handling for the sanity run
AC3 requires the committed JSON to record recall over positives — a partial run would produce misleading metrics
findings.finding: The new eval_solver container-arm code path (extended to call pass1_container when a fixture carries a `children:` list) has no described error handling. If the live LLM call fails mid-sanity run, the plan does not specify whether the committed decomp_shape_sanity.json is left in partial/corrupt state, or how the runner surfaces the failure to CI.
scenarios: An LLM provider timeout occurs on case 3 of 8 during the sanity run; the JSON is written with 3 results but CI passes the AC check on whatever is present
A future re-run produces different partial results, making the committed JSON non-reproducible
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the AC or the 'Bounded sanity harness' paragraph: the sanity runner should fail-fast on any LLM error (no partial JSON committed) and surface the error to CI via non-zero exit; alternatively, wrap each case in try/except and record `{"error": "..."}` per-case with an explicit `"complete": false` flag.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the `decomp-shape` rubric (plan_review_decomp_shape.md) includes an explicit anti-FP guard distinguishing it from G5's `vertical_slice` sub-answer — e.g. 'decomp-shape fires on the PARTITION AXIS of an existing container decomposition; if G5 has already flagged the ticket for single-concern or horizontal-big-bang, suppress decomp-shape to avoid duplicating the same finding'.
evidence: Plan: 'layer-cake split — children partitioned by ARCHITECTURAL LAYER… Each child is un-demoable alone and the integration risk is deferred to the end'
G5 routing vertical_slice key (criteria_routing.json line ~283): 'Sequencing has a thin vertical-slice / evidence-gated MVP de-risking the riskiest piece first, not a horizontal big-bang'
G5 applies_at has no scope restriction — it runs on container tickets as well as leaves
ABSENCE: the plan contains no acknowledgment that G5.vertical_slice might already fire on the layer-cake shape, nor a rubric anti-overlap guard
findings.finding: G5 already has a `vertical_slice` checklist sub-answer ('Sequencing has a thin vertical-slice / evidence-gated MVP de-risking the riskiest piece first, not a horizontal big-bang') that fires on the same container artifact as the proposed `decomp-shape` layer-cake smell — both run on container tickets, both flag a horizontal/layer partition. The plan does not acknowledge this overlap or explain how the rubric will avoid producing redundant findings with G5 on the same container.
findings.location: Why section (layer-cake smell description) + criteria_routing.json G5.checklist.vertical_slice
scenarios: A container plan with a DB/service/UI child partition triggers both G5.vertical_slice (horizontal big-bang) and decomp-shape (layer-cake), producing two advisory findings on the same defect from different criteria — confusing to the plan author and inflating advisory noise.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the decomp-shape rubric: an ANTI-FP clause stating the criterion targets the partition axis of an ALREADY-DECOMPOSED container (>=2 children present), and that a finding is suppressed when G5 has already surfaced a vertical-slice concern on the same container — distinguishing the two by the GATE sub-answer `has_container_decomposition` (children already exist) vs G5's recommendation to decompose.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan (Scope or AC) whether the eval_solver container-arm extension is scoped exclusively to decomp-shape fixtures, or intentionally generalizes to G3/G4 — and if the latter, explain how it relates to the existing plan-review-container.eval.yaml path (additive? supersedes inline-only? different fixture format?).
evidence: Plan: 'This story extends that arm to run a container criterion via pass1_container when the fixture carries a children: list — a small, contained enhancement that makes decomp-shape (and G3/G4) calibratable through rebar criteria eval'
plan-review-container.eval.yaml already exists with 12 labeled G3/G4 cases and a gold_set for fidelity diffing
eval_solver.py:105-109: current raise guard says 'not runnable over an inline eval fixture' for container criteria
ABSENCE: the plan never states whether the new _run_criterion_case container branch co-exists with, replaces, or is scoped to only decomp-shape (not G3/G4)
findings.finding: The eval_solver container-arm extension the plan proposes ('extends that arm to run a container criterion via pass1_container when the fixture carries a children: list') enables G3 and G4 to be run via _run_criterion_case — but an existing eval spec (plan-review-container.eval.yaml, 12 labeled cases) already provides calibration for G3/G4 through a distinct eval path. The plan does not clarify whether the new _run_criterion_case arm is additive (a second independent path) or intended to supersede the existing container eval spec for G3/G4, risking a NIH duplication in the eval infrastructure.
scenarios: A developer runs `rebar criteria eval G3` after this story ships and gets a result through the new _run_criterion_case arm — but the canonical G3/G4 calibration is the plan-review-container.eval.yaml path (which uses a different format and scoring). The two paths may give conflicting recall signals for G3/G4, and it is unclear which is authoritative.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Cite or add a brief justification for the post-hoc *detection* approach for consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge (distinct from claude-task-master/Kiro's construction-time enforcement), or note explicitly in the rubric prompt that this is a novel detection heuristic with no published prior art — so the rubric author calibrates the benign-reading filter conservatively.
evidence: Plan: 'consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge — a child NAMES/CONSUMES an artifact a SIBLING produces… yet there is NO ordering dependency'
SOTA report §R3: 'claude-task-master lower-ID-only DAG; Kiro waves' cited as the source
claude-task-master parse-prd.json: 'Set appropriate dependency IDs (a task can only depend on tasks with lower IDs)' — a construction-time enforcement rule, not a text-scan detection heuristic
Kiro: 'builds a dependency graph… groups independent tasks into waves' — likewise construction-time
ABSENCE: no citation of a system that detects ordering-edge absence by scanning sibling plan prose post-hoc
findings.finding: The 'consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge' smell is attributed to claude-task-master's lower-ID-only DAG rule and Kiro's wave grouping as prior art, but those systems ENFORCE ordering at construction time — they do not DETECT its absence via post-hoc text scanning of sibling plan prose. The plan presents the detection pattern as grounded in this prior art, but the cited sources only prevent the problem during plan authoring, not find it after the fact. No prior art for the specific detection pattern (scanning sibling descriptions for named artifact references, cross-checking against depends_on/blocks edges) is cited or verified.
findings.location: Why section — 'consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge' smell definition; SOTA report §R3
scenarios: The rubric author writes the detection heuristic (scan child B's text for artifact names produced by child A) with no validated prior art for this approach, increasing the risk of high false-positive rates on legitimate infra/migration epics where artifact names appear in multiple children without needing an ordering edge (e.g., a config file name mentioned in two sibling children for reference, not consumption)
Without detection-side prior art, the rubric's 'benign-reading filter' has no empirical calibration baseline for the consumed-artifact smell, which is the higher-risk of the two checks
findings.suggested_fix: In the rubric prompt (`reviewers/plan_review_decomp_shape.md`), add an ANTI-FP note: 'A sibling that merely *references* an artifact name (for documentation/context) is not a consumer; the finding requires evidence the child operationally *depends on* the artifact's existence (calls it, imports it, reads it) and no ordering edge is present. This check is a novel detection heuristic — err toward advisory and benign-read.' Also update the Why section to note that the enforcement prior art is distinct from the detection approach being introduced.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge (or explicitly exclude) the local `CONTAINER_CRITERIA` copy in `parity.py:167` — either update it alongside `pass1.CONTAINER_CRITERIA`, or document that advisory-only container criteria are intentionally excluded from parity fidelity scoring.
evidence: parity.py:167: `CONTAINER_CRITERIA = ('G3', 'G4')` — a local constant, not imported from pass1
plan: 'Adding decomp-shape to CONTAINER_CRITERIA fans it out through that same seam with per-criterion + per-child attribution; nothing else changes.'
parity.container_fidelity_report and parity.attribution_accuracy both filter findings by `r.gold_criterion in CONTAINER_CRITERIA` using the local copy
Evidence class: Verified (observed in source)
findings.finding: parity.py defines its own local `CONTAINER_CRITERIA = ('G3', 'G4')` constant (parity.py:167) that is NOT imported from pass1.py. Adding `decomp-shape` to pass1.CONTAINER_CRITERIA will NOT update parity.CONTAINER_CRITERIA, silently excluding decomp-shape findings from `attribution_accuracy` / `container_fidelity_report` fidelity scoring. The plan does not mention this copy.
scenarios: If decomp-shape is later promoted to blocking and its findings need fidelity attribution scoring, parity.attribution_accuracy silently excludes them — giving misleading 100% attribution accuracy for decomp-shape findings.
A future story adding decomp-shape to parity.CONTAINER_CRITERIA as part of promotion may not realize the local copy exists and may only update pass1.CONTAINER_CRITERIA.
findings.suggested_fix: In the scope section or a plan note, state that `parity.CONTAINER_CRITERIA` intentionally excludes advisory-only container criteria from S4/S5 fidelity scoring, OR add `parity.py` to the Files list and update its constant to import from `pass1.py`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
